question: Define an equivalence relation. Provide the three properties that a relation must satisfy to be considered an equivalence relation and give a brief explanation of each.
expectedPoints: Reflexivity: Every element is related to itself (∀a, aRa).
Symmetry: If a is related to b, then b is related to a (aRb ⇒ bRa).
Transitivity: If a is related to b and b is related to c, then a is related to c (aRb ∧ bRc ⇒ aRc).

question: Explain the fundamental connection between equivalence relations and partitions. Describe how an equivalence relation on a set A naturally creates a partition of A, and conversely, how a partition of A gives rise to an equivalence relation.
expectedPoints: An equivalence relation R on a set A partitions A into disjoint equivalence classes, where each class is a subset of elements that are all related to each other.
The equivalence classes are the blocks of the partition. The set of all equivalence classes, the quotient set A/R, is the partition.
Conversely, given a partition of A, one can define an equivalence relation where two elements are related if and only if they belong to the same block of the partition.

question: Let R be the relation on the set of integers Z defined by aRb if and only if 5 divides (a² - b²). Prove that R is an equivalence relation. Then, describe the distinct equivalence classes of this relation and show how they form a partition of Z.
expectedPoints: Proof of equivalence: Prove R is reflexive (5 divides a² - a² = 0), symmetric (if 5 divides a²-b², it divides b²-a² = -(a²-b²)), and transitive (using properties of divisibility).
Find the equivalence classes: They are [0] = {a ∈ Z: a ≡ 0 mod 5}, [1] = {a ∈ Z: a ≡ 1 or 4 mod 5}, [2] = {a ∈ Z: a ≡ 2 or 3 mod 5}. Note: a² mod 5 can only be 0,1,4.
Demonstrate the partition: The classes [0], [1], and [2] are disjoint (an integer's square mod 5 has only one value) and their union is all of Z.
